messages.content: What is the position and department of the person mentioned as the Associate Professor on the first page of the Brain Anatomy Booklet?
The person is an Associate Professor of Neuroanatomy & Neurosurgery in the Faculty of Medicine and Health Sciences, Department of Clinical Medicine, at Macquarie University.
What distinct levels of analysis has the understanding of the human brain passed through?

The understanding of the human brain has passed through anatomical dissection, philosophical questioning, histological analyses, psychological theories, artistic representation of its anatomical structures, and modern neuroimaging.
What does the description of surgical approaches help with, according to the text?
It allows for three-dimensional understanding of the topographic anatomy of a region, bearing in mind the syntopy of anatomical structures (how they are related to each other).
Why might the use of eponyms be helpful in learning anatomy?
Eponyms help to understand and learn anatomy by offering an interesting continuum between history, research, and medicine in the practical clinical setting of a physician.
What frame does the Institute of Anatomy of the Medical University of Vienna offer for studying anatomy?
It provides a perfect frame where students can perform dissections on human bodies in the same rooms where celebrated anatomists produced famous anatomical engravings and textbooks.
What innovative technologies for radioanatomical research are accessible at the Medical University of Vienna?
Ultra-high-field (7 Tesla) magnetic resonance and tractography are accessible to visualize and 'dissect' the secrets of the brain in vivo.
How does Austria's cultural background contribute to anatomical studies?
The laws and cultural background of Austria allow for these studies, thanks to the generous contribution of many people who voluntarily donate their body for didactic and research purposes.
In which program are four days specifically designated for dissection of the human brain at the Medical University of Vienna?
In the actual program of anatomical studies, during the third block ('Organmorphologie III'), four days are specifically designated for the dissection of the human brain.
For what purpose was the present booklet written?

The booklet is intended to assist students in understanding brain anatomy and physiology during the short dissection time and in the course 'Brain Anatomy in neurosurgical perspective', contextualizing brain anatomy with various clinical remarks.
Why might the text appear 'disbalanced' in some parts?
Because only the most interesting points (concluded by discussion with students) are presented, and exhaustive descriptions (e.g., all nuclei of cranial nerves) have been intentionally skipped.
What language is used for anatomical terms in the booklet?
Anatomical terms are written in Latin according to the 'Terminologia Anatomica' (Federative Committee on Anatomical Terminology, 1998), as used at the Medical University of Vienna.
What is the 'secret' aim of the booklet?
The secret aim is to inject enthusiasm into students to approach the knowledge of the brain and ignite passion to deepen topics, providing future personal contributions to neuroscience.
What roles did Prof. Dr. Manfred Tschabitscher have according to the text?

He was a professor of Anatomy at the Medical University of Vienna for 35 years, a great neuroanatomist, a pioneer of neuroendoscopy in anatomy, and also served as the author's Maestro in the history of medicine, German teacher, mentor in anatomical research, supervisor of his PhD project in Clinical Neurosciences, consultant, and confidant.
Which universities' students and specialists have used this booklet according to the preface?
The booklet has been used by students and specialists at the Medical University of Vienna and the University of Toronto.
What does the author note about the spread of the manual?
The author notes that the manual spread unexpectedly 'computer-to-computer' and mouth-to-mouth in a relatively quick fashion, and was found on desks in several places around the world.
At what levels of training is the booklet designed to be studied?
The booklet can be read and studied at different levels of training, from medical to PhD students, from residents and registrars to specialists.
Which ancient anatomists are associated with eponyms and descriptions of anatomical structures?
Erasistratus (304 BC - 250 BC), Herophilus (335 BC - 280 BC), Alcmaeon (6th Century BC), and Hippocrates (460 BC - 375 BC).
Why did the Egyptians remove the brain during mummification?
Because the brain was initially considered an organ of secondary importance; they removed it via the nose and threw it away, while other organs were preserved in canopic jars.
Why did Galenic theories dominate the study of human anatomy for over 1500 years?
Because Galen's theories, based on observations in animals, were arbitrarily transposed to humans, leading to erroneous descriptions, yet they dominated for more than 1500 years.
Why do the cerebral lobes take their names from the overlying bones of the skull?

The cerebral lobes take their names from the overlying bones of the skull because they relate directly to those bones, such as the frontal lobe under the frontal bone and the occipital lobe under the occipital bone.
What is the cerebral falx, and where does it run?

The cerebral falx is a portion of dura mater that runs in the depth of the longitudinal fissure, separating the two hemispheres except in its more anterior part where the medial faces of the frontal lobes (cingulate gyri) face each other.
What are the two components of the neurocranium?
The neurocranium has two components: the 'hub' (calvaria) and the floor of the cranial cavity, defined as the skull base.
Which bones form the cranial vault, and which bone contributes partially?
The cranial vault is formed by the frontal, parietal, occipital, and temporal bones, with some contributions from the greater wing of the sphenoid bone.
Describe the two portions of the frontal bone and what they correspond to.
The frontal bone is formed by a vertical portion, which corresponds to the forehead, and a horizontal portion, forming the roofs of the orbital and nasal cavities.
What are the parts of the temporal bone mentioned in the content?

The temporal bone has a pars petrosa (border of the middle cranial fossa), a pars squamosa (concurring with the formation of the lateral cranial vault), a pars tympanica, as well as a mastoid process and styloid process.
Which bone contains the foramen magnum, and what is its location on the cranial vault?
The occipital bone contains the foramen magnum. It forms the posterolateral and posterior part of the cranial vault and contributes to the floor of the posterior cranial fossa.
How do the lobes of the brain generally get their names, and what is the exception?
The lobes of the brain take their name from the overlying bones, the only exception being the insula, which is buried in the lateral sulcus and has no direct relationship with the bone.
What passes through the holes (foramina) on the calvaria, and give an example of such a hole?
The holes on the calvaria are generally crossed by anastomotic veins between the meninges and the epicranic veins. An example is the parietal foramen.
Name the four major sutures of the calvaria and the bones they separate.

The coronal suture divides the frontal bone from the parietal bones. The sagittal suture divides the two parietal bones along the midline. The lambdoid suture divides the occipital bone from the parietal bones. The squamous suture divides the temporal bone from the frontal and parietal bones.
What is craniosynostosis, and what are two exemplary kinds of synostosis mentioned?

Craniosynostosis is the premature ossification of the interdigitations of the cranial sutures, causing deformation of the head and correlated with potential dysfunctions of brain development (in some genetic syndromes). Exemplary kinds are synostosis of the sagittal suture, causing marked reduction of the biparietal diameter, and synostosis of the coronal suture, resulting in plagiocephaly (flattened forehead).
Which bones form the skull base?

The bones that form the skull base are: frontal bones, sphenoid bone, ethmoid bone, temporal bones, and occipital bone. The anterior part of the exocranial surface is also formed by the maxillary and palate bones.
How is the skull base anatomically divided?

The skull base is anatomically divided into three parts (fossae): the anterior skull base (anterior cranial fossa), the middle skull base (middle cranial fossa), and the posterior skull base (posterior cranial fossa).
What are the boundaries of the anterior skull base?
The anterior skull base is delimited anteriorly by the frontal bone and the posterior wall of the frontal sinus, and posteriorly by the limen of the lesser wing of sphenoid bone.
What is the sella turcica, and what does it contain?
The sella turcica is the central part of the middle cranial fossa and is part of the sphenoid bone. The sellar compartment contains the hypophysis.
What is the diaphragma sellae and what crosses it?

The diaphragma sellae is a meningeal sheet that separates the sellar compartment from the suprasellar compartment (brain). It has an opening at the center called the ostium of the diaphragma sellae, which is crossed by the pituitary stalk.
Why is the sella turcica strategically important for neurosurgeons?
The sella turcica is strategically important because it is the main 'door' to access the pituitary gland and brain via endonasal approaches (endonasal transsphenoidal approaches).
What does the posterior cranial fossa contain?
The posterior cranial fossa contains the foramen magnum, which is crossed by the brain stem that continues downward into the spinal cord.
What are the specific structures on the exocranial surface of the skull base?
Specific structures on the exocranial surface include the pterygoid process (medial and lateral plates), the styloid process of the temporal bone, the tip of the mastoid, and the occipital condyles.
What is the risk of fractures of the sinus walls?

Fractures of the sinus walls result in free communication between the exo- and endocranial compartments, with risk of meningeal and/or brain infections, or meningeal fistulae, with cerebrospinal fluid leak.
What structures pass through the jugular foramen?
The jugular foramen is traversed by nerves IX, X, XI, and the convergence of the inferior petrous sinus and sigmoid sinus into the jugular vein.
What is the anterior perforated substance?
It is a zone of the basal brain crossed by several terminal arteries supplying the basal ganglia and the internal capsule.
What is the origin of the accessory nerve (XI) and through which openings does it pass?
The accessory nerve is formed by axons from the upper cervical spinal cord, ascending into the skull through the foramen magnum, and leaving the skull through the jugular foramen.
Which muscles does the accessory nerve innervate, and what are the symptoms of its lesion?
The accessory nerve innervates the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles. Lesions cause drop of the shoulder, downward and lateral rotation of the scapula, and weakness in head rotation.
Where does the hypoglossal nerve (XII) originate and how does it exit the skull?

The hypoglossal nerve originates from its nucleus in the medulla oblongata, emerges from the ventrolateral sulcus in several rootlets, and converges into the hypoglossal foramen anterior to the occipital condyle.
Which tongue muscle is not innervated by the hypoglossal nerve, and what happens with a peripheral lesion?

The palatoglossus muscle is innervated by the vagus nerve, not the hypoglossal. A peripheral lesion of the hypoglossal nerve causes the tongue to deviate toward the side of the inactive muscle due to contralateral innervation.
What is an epidural hematoma?

An accumulation of arterial blood under high pressure in the epidural space, often caused by a fracture of the calvaria or skull base along the middle meningeal artery, which is a neurosurgical emergency.
Why does compression of the cerebral peduncle cause contralateral hemiparesis?

Because the cerebral peduncle carries the pyramidal tract for innervation of muscles on the contralateral side of the body; its dislocation and compression against the free margin of the tentorium disrupts this tract.
What is the Kernohan phenomenon?

A rare condition where ipsilateral mydriasis is associated with ipsilateral weakness (instead of contralateral) due to excessive herniation and rotation of the brain stem compressing the contralateral cerebral peduncle.
What is a subdural hematoma and from a histological point of view, what is its correct name?

A subdural hematoma is an accumulation of blood below the dura mater, classically described between arachnoid and dura, but histologically it is more correctly called 'intradural' because it occurs between the different histologic layers of the dura mater.
What causes a subdural hematoma, especially in older patients?
Stretching and rupture of bridging veins, which can occur after mild head trauma due to paraphysiologic atrophy of the brain increasing the volume of the subdural space and stretching these veins.
What is a subarachnoid hematoma and what commonly causes it?

An accumulation of blood between the pia mater and arachnoid, generally caused by the rupture of arteries running in the subarachnoidal space, which can be lacerated by head trauma or due to congenital diseases like aneurysms.
What is the cerebral falx?
A fold of dura mater that descends vertically in the longitudinal fissure, dividing the cranial cavity into two symmetric cavities (right and left).
Which venous sinuses are associated with the cerebral falx?
The superior sagittal sinus lies in its rostral triangular-shaped fold, and the inferior sagittal sinus runs in its free caudal margin.
What is the cerebellar tentorium and what does it contain?

A meningeal 'tent' covering the cerebellum, attached anteriorly to the pars petrosa of the temporal bone (where the superior petrosal sinus runs) and posteriorly to the internal surfaces of the occipital bone (where the transverse sinus runs). It has a central anterior opening called the tentorial incisura, occupied by the brain stem.
What is the diaphragma sellae and what does it separate?

A horizontal meningeal fold attaching to the four clinoid processes, forming the roof of the sella turcica. It separates the supradiaphragmatic space from the infradiaphragmatic space, where the pituitary gland lies.
What are the ventricles of the brain?
Cavities in the brain filled with cerebrospinal fluid (CSF). There are two lateral ventricles, a third ventricle, and a fourth ventricle.
How does CSF circulate from the ventricles to the subarachnoid space?

From the lateral ventricles to the third ventricle via the foramen of Monro, then from the third to the fourth ventricle via the aqueduct, then through the foramina of Luschka and Magendie into the subarachnoid space.
Why are cisterns important for neurosurgeons?

Because 1) they are often filled by pathological conditions (e.g., tumors and aneurysms), and 2) they serve as surgical corridors to reach deep areas of the brain, avoiding the intraparenchymal route.
What is the sylvian cistern and what major artery crosses it?
Also known as the cistern of the lateral fissure, it allows communication between cisterns over the convexity and deeper sites. The middle cerebral artery crosses it.
What does the interpeduncular cistern contain?
The top of the basilar artery with its quadrifurcation (two posterior cerebral arteries and two superior cerebellar arteries), as well as the oculomotor nerve.
What is the ambient cistern and what structures cross it?

Located laterally to the mesencephalon at the level of the tentorial incisura, it crosses both supra- and infratentorial spaces. The posterior cerebral artery, basal vein (of Rosenthal), trochlear nerve, superior cerebellar artery, and optic tracts cross it.
What is the largest cistern of the intracranial cavity and what structures does it contain?

The largest cistern of the intracranial cavity is the dorsal cerebellomedullary cistern (cisterna magna). It contains the lower medulla, the lower cerebellar vermis, the lower parts of the two cerebellar hemispheres, the posterior inferior cerebellar arteries, and the vertebral arteries.
How can the cisterna magna be accessed for CSF sampling, and what is the current clinical status of this technique?
The cisterna magna can be reached via a suboccipital puncture for CSF sampling, but this hazardous technique no longer has clinical application.
What is the phylogenetic order of the neocortex compared to the paleocortex and archicortex?
Phylogenetically, the neocortex is the most recent development of the telencephalon, different from the paleocortex (e.g., olfactory cortex) and from the archicortex (hippocampal cortex).
How many layers does the archicortex have, and which brain structure is an example of it?
The archicortex has three layers, and an exemplary structure possessing archicortex is the hippocampus, which is in the limbic system.
What are the six typical layers of the neocortex, from surface to deeper layers?
The six layers are: I: Molecular layer, II: External granular layer, III: External pyramidal layer, IV: Internal granular layer, V: Internal pyramidal layer, and VI: Multiform layer.
What are Betz giant pyramidal cells, and where are they located?

Betz giant pyramidal cells are a special kind of large pyramidal cells found in lamina V (internal pyramidal layer). They are the first motoneurons of the pyramidal tract, giving rise to the axons forming the corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts.
What is Gennari's stria, and which part of the brain is it associated with?
Gennari's stria is the external stria of the calcarine cortex that is visible to the naked eye, and it is part of the visual cortex.
How is the cortex defined as 'agranular' or 'granular' based on layer distribution?

The cortex is defined as 'agranular' when there is a predominance of pyramidal cells (e.g., in the motor area), and as 'granular' when there is an abundance of granular cells (e.g., in the somatosensory areas).
What is Brodmann's map, and how many areas does it divide the cortex into?
Brodmann's map is a famous cytoarchitectonic map that divides the whole cortex into 52 areas, with each area involved in different functions.
What causes variability in the pattern of sulci and gyri on the brain's surface?

Variability in sulci and gyri patterns can occur not only between different individuals but also within the same brain, specifically between the two hemispheres, which exhibit anatomical and physiological differences such as the volumetric asymmetry of speech-related areas (larger in the dominant hemisphere).
How is the classic anatomical terminology of the four cerebral lobes on the convexity related to cranial bones?
The classic anatomic terminology of the four cerebral lobes on the convexity is based on the name of the overlying cranial bone (e.g., frontal lobe overlies the frontal bone).
Where is the insula located, and why is it considered the 'fifth' lobe?
The insula is considered the 'fifth' lobe and is 'buried' in the depth of the lateral fissure, requiring separation of the frontal from the temporal lobe to access it.
What is the primary purpose of identifying the most important sulci on the lateral surface of the brain?
The identification of the most important sulci is essential to delineate the borders of the cerebral lobes, as well as important anatomical landmarks and functional regions.
Describe the orientation of the lateral sulcus and which lobes it separates.
The lateral sulcus runs in an oblique direction, separating the temporal lobe (below) from the frontal lobe and parietal lobe (above).
Which brain region is located at the vertex between the pars triangularis and pars opercularis?

The vertex between the pars triangularis and pars opercularis indicates the point of access generally used to split the lateral fissure and reach deeper structures such as the insula, middle cerebral artery, and hippocampus.
What is the characteristic feature that differentiates the central sulcus from the precentral and postcentral sulci?
The central sulcus does not generally reach the lateral sulcus; it ends about 2 cm before it, leaving a bridge of cortex called 'plique de passage'.
How are the borders of the frontal, temporal, and parietal lobes determined using the lateral and central sulci?

The frontal lobe lies anteriorly to the central sulcus and superiorly to the lateral sulcus. The temporal lobe lies inferiorly to the lateral sulcus. The parietal lobe lies posteriorly to the central sulcus.
What is the third important sulcus for identifying lobes, and which lobes does it separate?
The third important sulcus is the parieto-occipital sulcus. It separates the parietal lobe (anterior to it) from the occipital lobe (posterior to it).
How can the insula be visualized?

To visualize the insula, it is necessary to apply a gentle supero-inferior splitting of the lateral sulcus to separate the frontal lobe from the temporal lobe. The insula lies deeply to these structures.
Which rami of the lateral sulcus compartmentalize the inferior frontal gyrus?
The anterior ramus and the vertical ramus of the lateral sulcus compartmentalize the inferior frontal gyrus into pars orbitalis, pars triangularis, and pars opercularis.
What is the clinical relevance of the identification of the different parts of the inferior frontal gyrus?

The apex of the three parts indicates the access point to the sylvian fissure, where neurosurgeons split the arachnoid to separate the frontal lobe from the temporal lobe for access to the insula and lateral cistern.
What is expressive Broca's aphasia?

Expressive Broca's aphasia is a condition resulting from lesions in Broca's area (such as tumors or vascular diseases) in the dominant hemisphere, typically the left, where the patient can understand words but cannot articulate them, leading to dysarthric speech.
What is the function of the precentral gyrus?
The precentral gyrus, or primary motor cortex, is involved in the motor function of the contralateral side of the body.
What is frontal lobe syndrome?

Frontal lobe syndrome results from bilateral lesions of the frontal lobes and consists of abulia (lack of motivation) and serious personality disturbances, as seen in patients after frontal leucotomies.
What is the location of the parietal lobe?
The parietal lobe is located between the central sulcus, the parietooccipital line, and the posterior ramus of the lateral sulcus.
What is the function of Wernicke's speech region, and what happens if it is lesioned?

Wernicke's speech region, approximately located in the supramarginal gyrus, is important for language comprehension. Lesions involving this region result in receptive (or fluent) aphasia, where the patient fluently articulates meaningless words.
What are the symptoms of Gerstmann syndrome?
Gerstmann syndrome includes anomia, alexia, acalculia, agraphia, finger agnosia, and inability to coordinate the left side with the right side.
Where are the transverse temporal gyri (of Heschl) located?
They are located on the surface of the posterior part of the superior temporal gyrus, visible by opening the lateral portion of the sylvian fissure.
What are two possible effects of lesions to the auditory cortex (areas 41 and 42 of Brodmann)?
Lesions may affect hearing (though unilateral lesions have little effect) or may cause auditory aphasia, where the patient can hear but cannot understand.
What major sulcus is located on the medial surface of the occipital lobe?
The most significant sulcus of the occipital lobe on its medial surface is the calcarine sulcus, where the granular cortex of the visual areas is located.
What are the specific functions and lesion-related dysfunctions of the occipital lobe mentioned in the content?

The functions of the occipital lobe are essentially visual, including color perception, shape processing, and three-dimensional reconstructions of objects. Lesions can cause central blindness, central color blindness (achromatopsia), selective difficulty identifying faces (prosapognosia), and dysfunction in perceiving movements (cortical akinetopsia).
What is the insula also known as?
The insula is also called the 'Island of Reil', as described by this author at the beginning of the 19th century.
What separates the anterior and posterior regions of the insula?
The central sulcus of the insula separates the anterior and posterior regions, and partially corresponds to the division between the anterior agranular insular cortex and the posterior granular part.
What defines the marginal portions of the insula?
The periinsular sulcus, also called the circular sulcus of Reil, defines the marginal portions of the insula, reaching the limen insulae.
What are some functions the insula is involved in?

The insula is involved in visceromotor control (e.g., gastrointestinal motility, respiration, heart control), viscerosensory functions (abdominal sensations, nausea), taste, olfaction, vestibular functions, somatosensory functions, and higher psychological functions.
How is the anterior insula functionally different from the posterior insula?

The anterior insula is connected with the orbito-frontal cortex and involved in the control of food sensory and olfactory information, while the posterior part is involved in certain somatosensory functions.
What structures can be observed on the medial surface of the brain according to the provided content?

On the medial surface of the brain, one can see the structures of the limbic lobe and the medial projections of the lobes, such as the paracentral lobule (part of the frontal and parietal lobes), precuneus and cuneus (part of the occipital lobe).
How was the limbic lobe originally defined by Broca?

The limbic lobe was originally defined by Broca as 'le grand lobe limbique', an arciform convolution surrounding the interhemispheric commissures and the upper brain stem, formed by the cingulate gyrus and the parahippocampal gyrus. It was anatomically based, not functionally.
Why was the limbic lobe originally called the rhinencephalon?
It was called the rhinencephalon because it was thought to be involved in the mechanisms of olfaction, although it is now known to be more involved in psychic and memory functions (Papez's circuit).
What is the limbic lobe considered in terms of evolutionary brain development?

The limbic lobe is considered a transitional zone between the most phylogenetically recent brain (neocortex) and the oldest brain (archicortex), and is essentially formed by a cortical structure called the mesocortex.
Which structures form the limbic system according to the content?

The limbic system is formed by the hippocampal formation and amygdala, septum pellucidum, hypothalamus, and the central olfactory system, which has diffuse connections with limbic and paralimbic structures.
How is the hippocampal gray matter characterized in terms of structure?

The hippocampal gray matter has a laminar differentiation, referred to as hippocampal cortex or archicortex, and the cornu Ammonis and dentate gyrus roll up one inside the other, forming two interlocking U-shaped laminae separated by the hippocampal sulcus.
What are the cytoarchitectonic fields of the cornu Ammonis, and which is considered 'the vulnerable sector' for hypoxia?

The cornu Ammonis is divided into three fields based on cytoarchitecture: CA1, CA2, and CA3. CA1 is called 'the vulnerable sector' due to its different sensitivity to hypoxia, while CA3 is considered the 'resistant sector'.
What is the margo denticulatus, and how is it described?

The margo denticulatus is a narrow segment of the dentate gyrus visible on the temporal surface, with a characteristic toothed appearance. It overlaps the subiculum and is separated by the superficial hippocampal sulcus and the sulcus fimbriodentatus.
What are the three segments of the hippocampus anatomically?
The three segments of the hippocampus are the head (anterior part), the body (middle part), and the tail (posterior part).
Which part of the lateral ventricle contains the intraventricular component of the hippocampal body?
The intraventricular component of the hippocampal body is part of the floor of the temporal horn of the lateral ventricle.
What covers the intraventricular side of the hippocampus except the head?
The choroid plexus, attached to the taenia choroidea and taenia fornicis, covers the whole intraventricular side of the hippocampus except the head.
What is the uncus, and why is its anatomy important clinically?

The uncus is the most medial part of the temporal lobe. Its anatomy is important clinically because it can herniate into the tentorial incisura, which is key to understanding intracranial hypertension syndromes.
What is selective amygdalohippocampectomy, and what percentage of mesial temporal lobe epilepsy cases are refractory to drugs?

Selective amygdalohippocampectomy is a neurosurgical operation that involves resection of the anterior hippocampus, lateral amygdala, and entire entorhinal cortex, with disconnection of medial temporal structures from the orbito-frontal cortex. Mesial temporal lobe epilepsy is refractory to antiepileptic drugs in 10% of cases.
What are the boundaries of the parahippocampal gyrus based on the content?
The parahippocampal gyrus is a C-shaped gyrus bounded by the uncus medially, the isthmus of the cingulate gyrus posteriorly, the collateral sulcus laterally, and the rhinal sulcus anteriorly.
Which structures lie between the parahippocampal gyrus and the hippocampal sulcus?
The parahippocampal gyrus envelops the hippocampus, the dentate gyrus, and the cornus ammonis, which are separated from it by the hippocampal sulcus.
Which nucleus of the amygdala is the principal recipient of sensory information from the neocortex?
The lateral nucleus of the amygdala is the principal recipient of sensory information from the neocortex, as it has the largest cell mass among the groups of nuclei.
What are the functions of the cortical and medial nuclei compared to the basal, lateral, and central nuclei of the amygdala?
The cortical and medial nuclei of the amygdala serve as olfactory centers, while the basal, lateral, and central nuclei have limbic functions.
What is the fornix, and how does it originate in the brain?
The fornix is an arciform bundle of fibers that begins in the hippocampus on each side of the brain as the fimbria.
What are the two tracts that arise from the mammillary bodies, and where do they project?

The two tracts are the mammillothalamic tract, which projects to the anterior thalamic nucleus as part of Papez's circuit, and the mammillotegmental tract, which reaches some nuclei of the brain stem in the tegmentum.
What is the dorsal part of the septum called, and what is its cavity known as?

The dorsal part of the septum is called the septum pellucidum, a membrane bounded by the basal surface of the corpus callosum and by the rostral convexity of the fornix. The cavity between its two laminae is defined as the cavum of the septum pellucidum.
Where is the septum verum located, and what does it contain?

The septum verum, the ventral component of the septum, is located rostral to the lamina terminalis (the anterior wall of the third ventricle) within the parateraminal gyrus. It contains some nuclei interconnected with the frontal lobe and the limbic system.
What is the shape of the cingulum, and which gyri is it connected to?
The cingulum is a C-shaped convolution around the corpus callosum, connected by several arms to the medial frontal, paracentral, precuneal, and parahippocampal gyri.
Where does the cingulum begin and how is it separated from surrounding structures?
The cingulum begins beneath the genu of the corpus callosum and continues above it, separated by the sulcus of the corpus callosum.
What is the indusium griseum and what covers it?
The indusium griseum is a thin neuronal lamina above the corpus callosum, covered on each side of the midline by the medial and lateral longitudinal striae.
Which gyri are found medially and laterally to the olfactory sulcus on the basal surface?

Medially to the olfactory sulcus, the gyrus rectus can be found. Laterally to it, the orbital cortex is divided by an H-shaped pattern of sulci into anterior, medial, posterior, and lateral orbital gyri.
What structures can be visualized in the center of the basal surface of the brain?

In the center of the basal surface, one can visualize the optic system (optic nerves, optic chiasm, and optic tracts), mammillary bodies, tuber cinereum, uncus, and the ventral surface of the brain stem.
What is the 'central core' definition of the basal ganglia?
The 'central core' definition of the basal ganglia includes all structures, both gray matter and white matter, located between the insula and the midline of the brain.
Which structures together form the lentiform nucleus?
The putamen and globus pallidus together form the lentiform nucleus, so named because they are lens-shaped on the coronal plane, even though they are different structures.
Which brain structure is an expansion of the head of the caudate nucleus and is involved in reward and addiction?
The nucleus accumbens is a medial expansion of the head of the caudate nucleus and is involved in the mechanisms of reward, pleasure, addiction, fear, and the placebo effect.
What is the globus pallidus divided by, and into which parts?
The globus pallidus is divided by the internal medullary lamina into two parts: an internal part and an external part.
What is notable about the claustrum's connections and function according to the content?

The claustrum is interconnected with all parts of the brain except the auditory areas. Its exact function is unknown, but Francis Crick speculated about its possible relationship with processes that give rise to integrated conscious percepts.
What is the basal nucleus of Meynert involved in, and what disease is its degeneration linked to?
The basal nucleus of Meynert is involved in extrapyramidal and cognitive functions. Its degeneration is linked to the dementia of Alzheimer's disease.
How does the pineal gland produce melatonin?

Melatonin production occurs during the night via a regulatory network involving the optic nerves, hypothalamus (suprachiasmatic nucleus), intermediolateral column of the thoracic spinal cord, and the superior cervical ganglion.
What is the thalamus and where is it located?
The thalamus is a large, ovoid nuclear complex in the diencephalon. Its main mass is the dorsal thalamus, while the reticular thalami belong to its ventral part.
Which thalamic peduncle connects the temporal, orbito-frontal, insular, and amygdaloid structures?
The inferior thalamic peduncle connects the temporal, orbito-frontal, insular, and amygdaloid structures (part of the ventral amygdalofugal pathway via the ansa peduncularis).
What separates the mediodorsal thalamic nucleus from the ventral and lateral groups?
The internal medullary lamina, a thin sheet of white matter, divides the mediodorsal thalamic nucleus from the ventral and lateral groups.
What is the ventral thalamus composed of?

The ventral thalamus is composed of a dense network of fibers (zona incerta and tegmental fields, known as fields H1 and H2 of Forel) and the thalamic reticular nuclei, all interconnected with the basal ganglia.
What is thalamic pain syndrome?
Thalamic pain syndrome is a condition involving the thalamus where a lesion results in pain that cannot be accurately localized by the patient on his body.
Where is the hypothalamus located within the brain?
The hypothalamus is the ventral part of the diencephalon, located in the lateral walls of the third ventricle, where the hypothalamic sulcus is found.
Why is the red nucleus named 'red'?
The red nucleus is named 'red' because of its color due to the high concentration of iron deposits, and it is also highly vascularized.
What are the main inputs and outputs of the red nucleus, and what is its primary function?

The red nucleus receives inputs from the contralateral cerebellum (mainly via the dentatorubro tract in the superior cerebellar peduncle) and from the ipsilateral motor cortex. It sends outputs mainly to the spine (rubrospinal tract) and cerebellum. The nucleus is mainly involved in motor control of the arms.
What two techniques can be used to study the white fibers of the brain?

The white fibers of the brain can be studied in vitro by means of certain special anatomical techniques of dissection or in vivo by specific sequences used in magnetic resonance, such as Diffusion Tensor Imaging (DTI) tractography.
What is the centrum semiovale?
The centrum semiovale is the white matter with the shape of a semi-ovoid structure, recognized in an axial section of the hemispheres above the corpus callosum.
What is the function of association fibers, and what is an example?
Association fibers interconnect different regions of cortex within the same hemisphere. An example is the U-fibers, which interconnect the cortex around a sulcus.
What is an exemplary projection system, and what does it form?
An exemplary projection system is the corticospinal tract, formed by the axons of the Betz's cells (fifth cortical layer of the motor strip). It contributes to the formation of the internal capsule.
What is the uncinate fascicle and how is it formed?
The uncinate fascicle is the main interconnection between the limbic and paralimbic structures. It is formed by several leaves, separated by the gray matter of the claustrum.
In what specific medical cases are modern neurosurgical 'disconnection' operations used?
Modern neurosurgical 'disconnection' operations are used in some specific cases of drug-resistant epilepsy, to disconnect and functionally isolate some epileptogenic areas of the brain.
What is the brain stem?

The brain stem is the intermediate structure between the spinal cord and the cerebellum and brain. It contains the main motor and sensory pathways, all nuclei of ten cranial nerves (III-XII), and vital centers such as cardiorespiratory regulatory centers.
What are the three parts of the mesencephalon?
The mesencephalon consists of the tectum (roof), tegmentum, and the most ventral part formed by the cerebral peduncles (cerebral crus) and substantia nigra.
Which signs characterize Horner's syndrome, and which lesion causes it in Wallenberg's syndrome?
Horner's syndrome consists of ptosis, myosis, and anhidrosis on the ipsilateral side, caused by a lesion of the sympathetic fibers.
What is the arbor vitae and why is it called that?
The arbor vitae refers to the white matter of the cerebellum, which has a high geometrical complexity resembling tree arborizations.
Which is the largest nuclear complex in the cerebellum and what are its main projections?
The dentate nucleus is the largest nuclear complex in the cerebellum. It projects its fibers mainly to the contralateral red nucleus via the superior cerebellar peduncle.
List the three pairs of cerebellar peduncles and their primary connections.

The three pairs of cerebellar peduncles are: the inferior cerebellar peduncle (connecting mainly to the spinal cord), the middle cerebellar peduncle (connecting to the pons), and the superior cerebellar peduncle (connecting to the red nucleus and thalamus).
What arteries support the anterior and posterior circulation systems of the brain's arterial supply?
The anterior circulation system is supported by the internal carotid arteries, and the posterior circulation system is supported by the vertebral arteries. Together they form the circle of Willis.
How does the origin of the common carotid artery differ between the right and left sides?
On the right side, the common carotid artery originates from the brachiocephalic trunk; on the left side, it originates directly from the aortic arch.
Through which structures does the internal carotid artery pass before entering the subarachnoid space?

The internal carotid artery passes through the pars petrosa of the temporal bone, reaches the cavernous sinus in the parasellar compartment, and after a C-shaped torsion in the 'siphon', finally enters the subarachnoid space.
Which artery connects the two anterior cerebral arteries, and what segments does it define?
The anterior communicating artery connects the two anterior cerebral arteries. It divides the anterior cerebral artery into a precommunicant segment (A1) and a postcommunicant segment (A2).
What historical surgical procedure involving the anterior choroidal artery was performed for Parkinson's disease, and what was its serendipitous outcome?

In the 1950s, neurosurgeon Cooper clipped the anterior choroidal artery during an operation intended for resection of the cerebral peduncle. The serendipitous outcome was that the patient's tremor and rigidity improved without neurological deficits, which led to investigation of the globus pallidus's role in extrapyramidal dysfunctions and later inspired functional neurosurgical operations like deep brain stimulation.
How are the anterior and posterior spinal arteries formed?
The anterior spinal artery is formed by the union of two small branches from the vertebral arteries. The two posterior spinal arteries descend as separated vessels.
What is the definition of an aneurysm as given in the content?
An aneurysm is a congenital lesion of the walls of the artery, meaning 'dilatation', which can rupture and cause bleeding in the subarachnoid cisterns.
What are the two therapeutic methods for occluding aneurysms mentioned?
Endovascular techniques (introduction of microcatheters to insert coils) and neurosurgical methods (microneurosurgical intracisternal techniques using a clip on the aneurysm neck).
Why do veins serve as more reliable anatomical markers of brain structures than arteries?
Veins are adherent to the brain while arteries pass through the cisterns, making veins more reliable anatomical markers in neuroradiological imaging.
What are the two main groups of veins in the brain?
The veins of the brain can be divided into superficial veins, draining mainly the cortex, and deep veins, draining the deep parts of the brain.
Which sinuses receive blood from the cortical surface veins?
Veins draining the cortical surface bring blood to the superior and inferior sagittal sinuses, sphenoparietal sinus and cavernous sinus, as well as to the sinuses around the tentorium.
Describe the shape and location of the superior sagittal sinus (SSS).

The SSS is a triangular-shaped channel running medially in the groove of the lamina interna of the calvaria, from anteriorly (behind the frontal sinus, at the level of the foramen caecum) to the confluence of the sinuses.
What are lacunae in relation to the superior sagittal sinus?
The dura mater adjacent to the SSS contains venous spaces called lacunae, which collect blood predominantly from the meningeal veins.
How is the superior sagittal sinus divided?

The SSS is divided into an anterior third portion (from the foramen caecum to the coronal suture), a middle third portion (from the coronal suture to the lambdoid suture), and a posterior third portion (from the lambdoid suture to the confluens sinuum).
Where is the confluens sinuum located and what does it connect?

The confluens sinuum is located beneath the internal occipital protuberance and it is the connecting point of the SSS, the straight sinus, and the occipital sinus (which is inconstant), draining into the transverse sinus of the left and right side.
What is the clinical consequence of occlusion of the posterior third of the SSS?
Occlusion of the posterior third portion of the SSS generally results in the death of the patient, due to venous infarction of the brain.
Do cerebral veins and sinuses have valves?
Cerebral veins and sinuses lack valves, so the direction of the flow is also dependent upon the position of the head.
Where does the inferior sagittal sinus run and what does it join?
The inferior sagittal sinus runs at the inferior border of the cerebral falx, above the corpus callosum, and joins the straight sinus.
Where does the straight sinus originate?
The straight sinus originates behind the splenium of the corpus callosum, where the inferior sagittal sinus joins it together with the great deep veins, forming the vena magna.
What is the course of the transverse sinus?

The transverse sinus begins at the internal occipital protuberance and reaches, after a curvilinear course along the internal surface of the occipital bone, the base of the pars petrosa of the temporal bone, joining the sigmoid sinus.
What is the general difference in drainage between the right and left transverse sinuses?
The right transverse sinus predominantly drains blood from the cortical surface of the brain, while the left one predominantly drains blood from the deep part of the brain.
Why are the right transverse sinus and right jugular vein usually bigger?
On the right side, the jugular vein drains in a straight way (not angulated) into the subclavian vein, so they are bigger than the contralateral ones.
Where is the cavernous sinus located and what does it drain?
The cavernous sinus is located laterally to the sella turcica and drains blood from the orbits (via the superior ophthalmic vein), sphenoparietal sinus, and superior and inferior petrosal sinuses.
Name the two anastomotic veins on the surface of the brain and what they connect.

The superior anastomotic vein (of Trolard) connects the SSS with the sylvian veins. The inferior anastomotic vein (of Labbé) connects the veins of the sylvian fissure with the transverse and sigmoid sinuses.
At what level do the thalamostriate vein, choroidal vein, and vein of the septum pellucidum converge?
They converge at the level of the interventricular foramen in the internal cerebral veins, where the velum interpositum is located.
What is cerebral venous sinus thrombosis and how is it treated?

Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis is the occlusion of veins occurring in diseases of coagulation, infections, or as a consequence of drugs like contraceptives. It can be treated with anticoagulants, management of associated diseases (e.g., intracranial hypertension and seizures), and endovascular thrombolysis.
What is the main goal of neurosurgical operative approaches according to the content?
The ideal main goal of neurosurgical approaches is to perform pure 'lesionectomy' without damage of the brain, as emphasized by Prof. Gazi Yasargil.
What is the pterional approach and who popularized it?
The pterional approach is a single approach used for the microneurosurgical treatment of several diseases, popularized by Prof. Gazi Yasargil.
How are brain tumors classified in neurooncology according to the content?
Brain tumors are defined as intrinsic (intraparenchymal, within the brain) or extrinsic (extracerebral, e.g., meningeal tumors, or tumors of the calvaria, skull base and/or cranial nerves).
What visualization tools are routinely used in neurosurgery as mentioned in the content?
The anatomical regions accessed by different operative approaches are visualized by means of a microscope and/or an endoscope, as routinely performed in neurosurgery.
What medical conditions was the pterional approach originally designed to treat?

It was designed for the microsurgical treatment of saccular aneurysms of the circle of Willis, frontoorbital and temporobasal arteriovenous malformations, cavernomas, and extrinsic and intrinsic tumors.
Why is the craniotomy called 'pterional'?

It is called pterional because it is centered on the anatomical landmark 'pterion', which is the point of intersection of sutures in the lateral cranial vault (junction of the greater wing of the sphenoid, squamous temporal, frontal, and parietal bones).
Why is the reflection of the temporal muscle away from the bone a crucial point of the operation?
It is crucial because damage to the frontal branch of the facial nerve during this step can result in bad cosmetic and functional results for the patient.
What technique is used to preserve the frontal branch of the facial nerve during the pterional approach?
The interfascial approach, which involves working between the layers of the superficial fascia temporal, is used to preserve the nerve.
Which anatomical structures can be identified after dural opening in the pterional approach?

The frontal and temporal lobes, arachnoid membrane, sulci and gyri of the inferior frontal gyrus and superior temporal gyrus (e.g., pars orbitalis, pars triangularis, pars opercularis, superior temporal sulcus), and arteries and veins can be identified.
List five main anatomical structures visible after opening the sylvian cistern completely.
Middle cerebral artery (with bifurcation at the limen insulae), internal carotid artery, anterior cerebral artery (A1 and A2), anterior communicating artery, and optic nerve.
What is the retrosigmoid approach used to access?

The retrosigmoid approach is used to reach the posterior cranial fossa, specifically the pontocerebellar angle, which is the region between the pars petrosa of the temporal bone (anteriorly), the cerebellum (posteriorly), and the brain stem (medially), along with its related cisterns and neurovascular structures.
Which two conditions can be treated surgically using the retrosigmoid approach?

The retrosigmoid approach can be used to surgically resect vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma) and to treat trigeminal neuralgia caused by neurovascular conflict (e.g., compression of the trigeminal nerve by a cerebellar artery).
Where is the craniotomy performed in relation to the sigmoid sinus and which bone landmark is useful?

The craniotomy is performed behind the sigmoid sinus (behind the ear and upon the mastoid process of the temporal bone). A useful landmark is the asterion, where the lambdoid, parietomastoid, and occipitomastoid sutures converge.
What does the asterion generally overlie, and how is it used safely?
The asterion generally overlies the convergence of the transverse sinus with the sigmoid sinus, and a burr hole can be safely performed just inferior to it.
List the major structures that can be visualized through the microscope or endoscope during a retrosigmoid approach.

The structures that can be visualized include all cranial nerves of the posterior cranial fossa (from the third to the 12th), cerebellar arteries, tentorium, basilar artery, vertebral artery, internal auditory meatus, and jugular foramen.
What is the purpose of the interhemispheric transcallosal approach?
The purpose is to access the corpus callosum and, through a small incision in its fibers, visualize the contents of the third ventricle.
Which structures are visualized in the interhemispheric cistern during this approach?

The contents visualized include the callosamarginal artery, pericallosal artery (the continuation of the anterior cerebral artery over the corpus callosum), the cingulate gyrus on the medial surface of the hemisphere, and the corpus callosum.
How is access to the interhemispheric cistern achieved in this procedure?
Access is achieved by performing a craniotomy near the midline close to the coronal suture, then retracting the medial surface of the frontal lobe laterally away from the cerebral falx.
What structures are visualized inside the sphenoid sinus after enlarging the sphenoidal ostium?
The sellar floor, clivus, planum sphenoidalis, and the bony protuberances of the internal carotid artery and optic nerve, which form the opticocarotid recess.
After opening the sellar floor, what is visualized?
The pituitary gland enveloped in its connective tissue, and above it the diaphragma sellae, the anterior part of the circle of Willis, and the optic nerves.
What is the purpose of executing brain biopsies?
The execution of brain biopsies is indicated to determine the diagnosis of intracranial lesions, in order to assess the best treatment for the patient.
How are the target point and entry-point identified in stereotactic biopsies?

The target point (the lesion) and the entry-point (the 'gate' on the skull to access the lesion) are identified by means of Cartesian coordinates, acquired on images (computed tomography or magnetic resonance).
What are the two types of stereotactic biopsy methods mentioned?
The biopsy can be 'frame-based' (using a stereotactic frame fixed to the skull by screws in local anesthesia) or 'frameless', using neuronavigation systems.
What is the relevance of three-dimensional syntopy knowledge in stereotactic biopsy?

Knowledge of the three-dimensional syntopy of the brain (white matter, nuclei and vessels) is essential to reach the target while avoiding damage to important and/or vital structures along the trajectory.
What is the definition of hydrocephalus?

Hydrocephalus refers to an excess of 'water in the brain', meaning excess of cerebrospinal fluid in the ventricular system, with the consecutive enlargement of the ventricles (ventriculomegaly) and compression of the brain parenchyma.
How does an obstruction of the aqueduct of Sylvius affect the ventricles?

An obstruction of the aqueduct of Sylvius causes enlargement of the two lateral and the third ventricle because CSF produced in the choroid plexus of the supratentorial compartment cannot flow into the fourth ventricle.
What is the cause of communicating hydrocephalus?
In communicating hydrocephalus, no visible obstruction is apparent, and the enlargement of the ventricles is due to normal production of CSF and abnormal CSF reabsorption.
What can cause communicating hydrocephalus after subarachnoid bleeding?
Communicating hydrocephalus can occur following a subarachnoid bleeding when the products of degradation of hemoglobin obstruct the normal pathway of reabsorption of CSF (i.e., the capillaries).
Why is urinary incontinence a symptom of normal pressure hydrocephalus?
Frontal lobes are the site of a center for the control of urinary function, so compression can explain urinary incontinence.
Why does ataxia of deambulation occur in normal pressure hydrocephalus?

Ataxia occurs because the internal capsule is adjacent to the lateral ventricles, and fibers to spinal motoneurons for the legs are located medially near the lateral wall of the lateral ventricles. Lateral expansion of the ventricles compresses the pyramidal tract, causing ataxia.
How does a ventriculoperitoneal shunt work?

A small catheter is inserted in the frontal or occipital horn of the lateral ventricle and brought subcutaneously to the abdomen to drain CSF into the intraperitoneal cavity, with a pressure-valve to control outflow.
What anatomical landmarks are visible when inserting an endoscope into the lateral ventricle?
The landmarks include the foramen of Monro, choroid plexus, thalamostriate vein, choroidal vein, vein of the septum pellucidum, columns of the fornix, and head of the caudate nucleus.
What structures are visible after opening the floor of the third ventricle during the procedure?
Visualization of the clivus and the contents of the interpeduncular cistern: the basilar artery with its terminal quadrifurcation and oculomotor nerve.
Who is the author of 'The Human Hippocampus' and what is the book known for?

The author is Duvernoy HM, and the book is known for being a compact and extensive textbook on the functional anatomy and radioanatomy of the hippocampus, including many amazing radiologic and histologic pictures.
What is notable about the book 'Neuroanatomie' by Firbas, Gruber, and Mayr?
It is an extensive summary of neuroanatomy written in the German language by the professors of Anatomy of the Medical University of Vienna.
Which anatomy textbook is referred to as the 'bible' of anatomy for generations of physicians and which chapter is suggested to read?
Gray's Anatomy is referred to as the 'bible' of anatomy, and it is suggested to read the chapter about the basal ganglia.
What is the 'Handbook of Neurosurgery' by Greenberg MS known for among neurosurgery residents?
It is known as the inseparable companion of each resident in neurosurgery all over the world, with a small format to be kept in the white coat.
What is the CIBA Collection of Medical Illustrations by Netter FH known for?
It is known as the most didactic collection of images and schemas to illustrate the correlations between clinical, descriptive and functional neuroanatomy.
In 'The Human Central Nervous System' by Niewenhuys et al., which chapters are highly recommended to read?
It is highly recommended to read the section on the neocortex (chapter 15 of the fourth edition) and the limbic system (chapter 23).
Name two specific volumes of Yasargil's 'Microneurosurgery' that are highly recommended and what do they cover?

Volume I is highly recommended for a detailed description of the anatomy of the cisterns and arteries of the brain, and Volume IVA is recommended for topographic anatomy of the brain and a description of some operative approaches.
Who are the individuals credited for collecting images and providing specimens?
Prof. Dr. Manfred Tschabitscher is thanked for his help, with images and specimens credited to © A. Di Ieva 2011 and © M. Tschabitscher.
What are the main topics covered on page 59 of the Brain Anatomy Booklet?

The page covers the bones of the skull base, foramina of the skull base, 3D CT of the skull base, cranial nerves I and II, and the right parasellar compartment (cavernous sinus) with related cranial nerves.
Which cranial nerves and anatomical compartment are specifically mentioned on page 59?
Cranial nerves I and II are mentioned, along with the right parasellar compartment (cavernous sinus) and its related cranial nerves.
Which cranial nerve reaches the posterior wall of the cavernous sinus and is noted to be crossed by a small artery?
The abducens nerve reaches the posterior wall of the cavernous sinus, and it is noted that the nerve is crossed by a small artery.
